audit_status.review_note: Reviewed for Joseph's favored status, kethonet passim, dreams, brothers' hatred, pit, sale to Ishmaelites/Midianites, dipped garment, and Jacob's mourning. Compact verse layer for app ingestion.

verses.restored: Jacob dwelled in the land of his father’s sojournings, in the land of Canaan.
These are the generations of Jacob. Joseph, seventeen years old, was shepherding the flock with his brothers, and Joseph brought a bad report of them to their father.
Israel loved Joseph more than all his sons, because he was a son of his old age, and he made him a long ornamented tunic.
His brothers saw that their father loved him more than all his brothers, so they hated him and could not speak peaceably to him.
Joseph dreamed a dream and told it to his brothers, and they hated him even more.
He said to them, Please hear this dream that I have dreamed.
Look, we were binding sheaves in the field, and my sheaf rose and stood upright; and your sheaves gathered around and bowed down to my sheaf.
His brothers said to him, Will you really reign over us? Will you truly rule us? They hated him even more because of his dreams and his words.
He dreamed another dream and told it to his brothers, saying, Look, I have dreamed another dream: the sun, the moon, and eleven stars were bowing down to me.
He told it to his father and brothers, and his father rebuked him and said, What is this dream? Shall I, your mother, and your brothers really come to bow ourselves to the ground before you?
His brothers envied him, but his father kept the matter.
His brothers went to shepherd their father’s flock in Shechem.
Israel said to Joseph, Are not your brothers shepherding in Shechem? Come, and I will send you to them. He said, Here I am.
He said to him, Go now, see the peace of your brothers and the peace of the flock, and bring me word. So he sent him from the valley of Hebron, and he came to Shechem.
A man found him wandering in the field, and the man asked him, What are you seeking?
He said, I am seeking my brothers. Please tell me where they are shepherding.
The man said, They have moved from here, for I heard them say, Let us go to Dothan. Joseph went after his brothers and found them in Dothan.
They saw him from afar, and before he came near to them they conspired against him to make him die.
They said to one another, Look, this master of dreams is coming.
Now come, let us kill him and throw him into one of the pits, and we will say a wild animal devoured him. Then we shall see what becomes of his dreams.
Reuben heard and rescued him from their hand, saying, Let us not strike his life.
Reuben said to them, Shed no blood. Throw him into this pit in the wilderness, but do not lay a hand on him, so that he might rescue him from their hand and return him to his father.
When Joseph came to his brothers, they stripped Joseph of his tunic, the long ornamented tunic that was on him.
They took him and threw him into the pit. The pit was empty; there was no water in it.
They sat down to eat bread. They lifted their eyes and saw a caravan of Ishmaelites coming from Gilead, with camels carrying spices, balm, and myrrh, going down to Egypt.
Judah said to his brothers, What gain is there if we kill our brother and cover his blood?
Come, let us sell him to the Ishmaelites, and let not our hand be upon him, for he is our brother, our flesh. His brothers listened.
Midianite traders passed by. They drew Joseph up from the pit and sold Joseph to the Ishmaelites for twenty pieces of silver, and they brought Joseph to Egypt.
Reuben returned to the pit, and look, Joseph was not in the pit. He tore his garments.
He returned to his brothers and said, The boy is gone, and I, where can I go?
They took Joseph’s tunic, slaughtered a male goat, and dipped the tunic in the blood.
They sent the long ornamented tunic and brought it to their father, saying, We found this. Recognize, please, whether it is your son’s tunic or not.
He recognized it and said, It is my son’s tunic. A wild animal has devoured him; Joseph is surely torn apart.
Jacob tore his garments, put sackcloth on his loins, and mourned for his son many days.
All his sons and all his daughters rose to comfort him, but he refused to be comforted and said, I will go down to Sheol mourning to my son. His father wept for him.
The Midianites sold him in Egypt to Potiphar, an officer of Pharaoh, captain of the guard.
